segments.s453-g97: فرستاد قیصر: verb-before-subject order is common in classical Persian poetry; فرستاد is the past tense verb 'sent' and قیصر is the subject 'Caesar'. ز هر گونه چیز is a prepositional phrase (ز + NP) functioning as the thing sent: ز 'of/from' + هر گونه 'every kind of' + چیز 'thing'.
segments.s453-g98: This is a compact list of additional items. برده‌ها is the plural noun 'slaves' (here: slave‑girls) and بدره is a poetic noun meaning 'beauty' or 'full‑moon' used as ‘beautiful ones’; بسیار is a quantifier 'many'; نیز is the enclitic conjunction 'also'. ابا is an unusual/archaic item in this position and does not match the provided senses precisely, so it functions as another nominal item in the list and is best treated as lexically uncertain here.
segments.s453-g99: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed' and پذیرفت is the past tense verb 'accepted'. زو is a fused preposition + pronoun (ز + او) meaning 'from him'. آنچ بود is a free relative clause 'that which there was' (آنچ = 'that which', بود = past copula 'was') serving as the object of پذیرفت.
segments.s453-g100: This short clause lists the valuables and gives a final comment. ز دینار introduces 'from (the) dinars'; وز here links the two nouns in classical/poetic usage (the provided lexical senses don't supply a direct match, so it functions as a linker/conjunction in context). گوهر 'gem/jewel' is the second noun. نابسود is a predicate/adjective placed after the nouns to qualify the acceptance (literally 'not fruitless' → contextually 'he was not displeased/was not dissatisfied'); poetic word order places the adjective after the nouns it qualifies.
segments.s453-g41: وزان is a prefatory adverbial 'from that (place/time)'; جایگه 'place/spot' is the locative noun; شد is the past verb (here marking movement/turning) and combines with سوی + میمنه to mean 'turned/moved toward'; سوی is the preposition 'toward' and میمنه is the old/formal word for 'the right side' (direction). The phrase follows classical/poetic word order where the adverbial (وزان) precedes the locative phrase.
segments.s453-g42: بیاورد is past narrative 'he brought' (bi‑prefix common in epic narration); چندی is a quantifier 'some/several'; سلیح 'weapons' is the direct object; و links the two nouns; بنه here means a small bundle/heap (poetically 'baggage' or 'bundles'). Word order is Verb + quantified objects, typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s453-g43: همه لشکر روم is the subject 'the entire Roman army/host'; برهم is an adverb/adjectival complement meaning 'in disorder / broken up'; درید (past of دریدن) is the verbal predicate 'was torn / was broken up / was scattered'. The compact order (subject + adverbial + verb) is characteristic of epic diction.
segments.s453-g44: کسی here is used with the negative verb ندید to mean 'no one'; از یلان is a partitive/genitive 'of the heroes'; خویشتن را is the emphatic object 'himself' with the object marker را; ندید is the past negated 'did not see'. The clause literally reads 'no one of the heroes saw himself.'
segments.s453-g45: دلیران ایران is the subject 'the brave ones of Iran' (Iranian heroes). به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner/behavior of'; کردار is 'behavior/conduct' and شیر is the simile 'lion'. Together they form a simile: 'in the manner of a lion.'
segments.s453-g46: همی is a classical adverb indicating continuous or emphatic aspect (here 'still/continually'); تاختند is the past plural 'they charged/attacked'; از پس functions as 'after/behind' (in pursuit of); اندر is the locative 'into/among'; دلیر is 'the brave one(s)'. Together the phrase means they continued to rush on, in pursuit, into the thick of the fighting.
segments.s453-g47: بکشتند is the past plural narrative 'they killed'; چندان is a quantifier 'so much / so many' indicating the extent of killing; ز marks source/partitive 'of/from'; رومی سپاه 'the Roman host/army' is the noun phrase affected. Poetic order places the quantifier and source after the verb.
segments.s453-g48: که introduces a result clause ('so that'); گل شد is 'became (a) flower'—a metaphorical turn saying the ground was like a flower; ز خون = 'from blood' (ablative/source); خاک آوردگاه is a genitival/compound 'the soil of the battlefield' (آوردگاه = place of bringing/battlefield). The clause uses inverted, compact epic word order: 'so that from blood the battlefield soil became a flower.'
segments.s453-g17: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' placed before the prepositional phrase. تا here means 'as far as / up to' and combines with به + noun to mark the limit or destination: 'تا به لشکرگه رومیان' = 'as far as / up to the Romans' camp.' Note the word order Adverb + تا + به + place (لشکرگه رومیان).
segments.s453-g18: همی is an archaic/poetic preverbal particle indicating continuous or repeated action ('kept / was continually'). تاخت is the past tense verb 'rushed/galloped' and with the preposition بر expresses attack/charge against. سان introduces a simile ('like'), and شیر ژیان is a noun phrase where ژیان (from 'life') functions adjectivally to mean 'living' — 'a living lion.'
segments.s453-g19: This is a copular/result clause: زمین شد ('the ground became'). ز رومی indicates cause/agent ('because of / by the Romans'). چو introduces the simile that follows: چو دریای خون = 'like a sea of blood.' Note the order: subject (زمین) + شد (became) + cause (ز رومی) + simile (چو ...).
segments.s453-g20: This clause follows the previous with a dative/topic marker: جهانجوی را places the ambitious/world‑seeking man as the affected person. تیغ شد رهنمون is an inverted predication meaning 'the sword became (شد) a guide (رهنمون).' The structure is 'X را Y شد Z' = 'for X, Y became Z.'
segments.s453-g21: به پیروزی marks the result/state 'in victory' or 'with victory.' از رومیان indicates source ('from the Romans'). گشت باز is a compound verbal idea meaning 'turned back / returned' (گشت + باز = 'came back'). Word order places the result/state before the motion verb: 'به پیروزی ... گشت باز' = 'he returned in victory.'
segments.s453-g22: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase meaning 'toward/near' and governs the following noun phrase 'سالار گردنفراز'. Here سالار is a title 'commander' and گردنفراز is an epithet/name attached to it; together they mean 'the commander Gerdnefaraz' or 'the proud commander.' The whole phrase indicates direction/place: 'toward the commander G.'
segments.s453-g23: بسی functions as an intensifier meaning 'much/many' before the noun آفرین ('praise/acclamation'). یافت is the past-tense verb 'received/obtained.' از رشنواد marks the source 'from Reshnvad.' The clause literal order is 'much praise he obtained from Reshnvad.'
segments.s453-g24: که introduces a wished-for clause here (optative) followed by the sentence expressing the wish. این لشکر شاه = 'this army of the king' (لشکر + genitive شاه). بی‌تو = 'without you.' مباد is an archaic optative negative meaning 'may it not be' or 'let it not be so.' Together: 'که این لشکر شاه بی‌تو مباد' = 'that this king's army may never be without you.'
segments.s453-g33: بهم (to one another) + بازخوردند (past, 3rd pl.) forms a reciprocal action: the verb is plural past 'they struck back / clashed' and 'بهم' marks the action as mutual. هر دو سپاه follows as the subject phrase in natural word order (lit. 'each two hosts' = 'both hosts').
segments.s453-g34: شد is the past tense 'became' (or 'came to be'); از گرد خورشید تابان سیاه is a circumstantial phrase explaining the result or appearance: از گرد = 'around/from the circle of', خورشید تابان = 'the shining sun', and سیاه is the predicate adjective 'black/dark' that the subject (implicit atmospheric appearance) assumed. The clause means a blackness gathered/appeared around the shining sun.
segments.s453-g35: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'; داراب (name) + پیش آمد is a normal S + adverb + verb order meaning 'Darab came forward'. و حمله برد is coordinate: حمله 'an attack' + برد (poetic collocation) meaning 'he made/launched' the attack — برد here functions like 'carried out' rather than 'gain'.
segments.s453-g36: عنان را uses the direct-object marker را with عنان 'the reins' (object). به + اسپ تگاور marks the indirect recipient: 'to the steed (اسپ) [characterized as] تگاور' — تگاور here is an epithet ('bold/warrior-like') modifying اسپ. سپرد is past 'he handed/entrusted (over)'.
segments.s453-g37: به پیش (literally 'to the front of') + صف رومیان ('the line/formation of the Romans') sets the location 'in front of the Roman ranks'. کس نماند is an existential negative: کس here functions as 'anyone/no one' and نماند is past 'remained/was left' — together 'no one remained'.
segments.s453-g38: ز (poetic 'from') گردان شمشیرزن = 'from the companies/squadrons of swordsmen' (گردان = troop/unit, شمشیرزن = swordsman). بس نماند is a negative quantity statement: بس 'enough' with نماند 'remained not' yields 'there remained not enough' or idiomatically 'hardly any remained'.
segments.s453-g39: به قلب سپاه 'to the heart/core of the army' gives the goal; اندر آمد is an archaic/prepositional sequence meaning 'entered in' (اندر = 'in/into', آمد = 'came/entered'). چو گرگ is a simile 'as/like a wolf' modifying the (implied) subject who enters.
segments.s453-g40: پراگنده is an adjective/participial form 'scattered/widely spread' used with کرد (past 'did/made') to mean 'caused to be scattered' or simply 'scattered'. آن سپاه بزرگ is the direct object 'that great army' (آن = 'that', سپاه بزرگ = 'great army').
segments.s453-g73: This is a subordinate temporal clause introduced by چو ("when"). Word order is typical older Persian: temporal marker + prepositional phrase از باختر ("from the west") + predicate تیره شد (past tense "became dark") + the topical noun phrase روی مهر ("the face of Mehr/the sun"), which follows the verb for poetic inversion. شد is simple past ('became').
segments.s453-g74: بپوشید is a past tense verb (he/it put on, covered itself). The direct object/phrase دیبای مشکین ("dark/musk‑colored brocade") precedes the subject سپهر ("sky/heavens")—a common poetic inversion where the verb comes before its subject. مشکین describes دیبا (adjectival order 'adjective after noun' is normal in Persian poetry).
segments.s453-g75: همان پاس is a demonstrative/identifying noun phrase ('that same guard' / 'the very sentry'). از تیره شب is a prepositional phrase ('out of/from the dark night'). درگذشت here functions as a past verb 'passed (through / by)' — the classical verb درگذشت can mean 'passed away' or more generally 'passed by/through'; the clause is simple past with poetic concision.
segments.s453-g76: This is a noun phrase describing the troop disposition: طلایه پراگنده ("the vanguard, scattered") followed by the locative phrase بر گرد دشت ("around the plain"). بر + گرد together yield the sense 'around' (گرد here is the noun/adverb 'around'). The clause is verbless in form and appositive to the previous action.
segments.s453-g77: غو پاسبان is a genitive/attributive sequence ('the uproar of the sentries' or 'the sentries' uproar'). خاست is a past tense verb 'arose'. چون introduces the simile 'like an earthquake' (زلزله). Word order places the noun phrase before the verb and the comparative phrase at the end, typical in classical verse.
segments.s453-g78: همی is a classical particle adding emphasis ('indeed' / ongoing sense); شد is past 'became' or 'was turned into'. چو introduces the comparison to اواز شیر یله ('the sound/cry of a wild/unrestrained lion'). Note the adjective یله follows شیر, a common poetic adjective order.
segments.s453-g79: Another temporal clause introduced by چو ('when'). زرین سپر ('golden shield') is the direct object of برگرفت (past 'took up / lifted'), and آفتاب ('the sun') is the agent placed at the end for poetic effect. برگرفت is simple past, here 'raised' or 'lifted up'.
segments.s453-g80: سر جنگجویان is a genitive noun phrase ('the heads of the warriors' / idiomatically 'the warriors'). برآمد is past 'arose' (they awoke/raised up), followed by the preposition ز ('from', here classical form of از) and خواب ('sleep'). The clause describes the warriors emerging from sleep as a simple past event.
segments.s453-g65: This segment begins with نگه‌دار, an imperative/archaic verbal form meaning “keep” or “hold” (imperative directed to the addressee). It takes the following noun phrase چیزی as its direct object, which is modified by the relative clause introduced by که + رای آیدت (که = 'that/which'; رای آیدت = 'the counsel/plan comes to you' with the clitic -ت = 'to you'). Word order: Imperative verb + object + relative clause.
segments.s453-g66: ببخش is an imperative meaning roughly 'give (away)' or 'forgo/let go' here; it governs the following nominal clause آنچ + دل رهنمای آیدت. آنچ (= 'that which') introduces a free relative, and دل رهنمای is a poetic compound read as 'the heart's guide' (guide of the heart); آیدت again is 'comes to you' (present/past sense depending on context). The whole segment: 'Grant/let go that which the heart's guide brings to you.'
segments.s453-g67: هرآنچ آن is a fused demonstrative + indefinite meaning 'whatever of that' or 'whatever (of it)'. پسندت ('your liking/approval') is the internal predicate of the subordinate clause, followed by نیاید, a negative present/past form 'does not come/does not please'. The main imperative ببخش ('give up/forgo') follows, so overall: 'Whatever of that does not please you, give it up.'
segments.s453-g68: تو is the emphatic subject pronoun 'you'. نامی‌تری is the comparative adjective 'more renowned' (-تری = comparative). از introduces the comparison ('than'), and خداوند رخش is a genitive/possessive phrase 'the lord/master of Rakhsh' (رخش = the steed's name). Literal order: 'You are more renowned than the lord of Rakhsh.'
segments.s453-g69: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آن دید = 'saw that' (دید = past 'he saw'), and داراب شد شادکام is the main clause: داراب (proper name) + شد (past of 'become') + شادکام (adjective 'joyful/fortunate'). Word order: temporal subordinate clause (چو + clause) precedes the main clause.
segments.s453-g70: یکی functions as an indefinite 'one/someone' (subject). نیزه برداشت is a verbal phrase: نیزه (spear) + برداشت (past verb 'took up', lit. 'lifted'). از بهر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of' and نام here means 'name' in the sense of 'reputation, fame'. The whole: 'Someone took up a spear for the sake of fame.'
segments.s453-g71: فرستاد is the past tense verb 'he sent/dispatch(ed)'. دیگر سوی رشنواد is an adverbial phrase: دیگر = 'another' (i.e., another person), سوی = 'toward', and رشنواد is the place/name of the addressee. Word order here places the verb before the adverbial directional phrase: 'He sent another toward Reshnawad.'
segments.s453-g72: بدو گفت is a two-part phrase: بدو = 'to him' (dative), گفت = '(he) said'. What follows is a wish/blessing: پیروز بادی و شاد is an optative formula—بادی is an archaic/poetic optative/subjunctive form meaning 'may you be' (from بودن), so پیروز بادی و شاد = 'may you be victorious and joyful.'
segments.s453-g25: چو functions as the temporal conjunction 'when'. ما is the first‑person plural subject. بازگردیم is a present/subjunctive form used here for a future/temporal meaning 'we return'/'we come back'. زین is an archaic/poetic form equivalent to 'from/of this' (not the literal 'saddle' sense listed) and links to رزم to mean 'from this battle/campaign'. رزم رَوْم (noun + proper name) means 'the battle/campaign of/against Rome'.
segments.s453-g26: سپاه is the subject 'the army/host'. اندر + آید is a classical word order (preposition before verb) where اندر conveys location/motion 'into/in' and آید is 3rd person present 'comes' (here future relative to 'when'). به آباد بوم is a prepositional/dative phrase giving destination; به + آباد بوم = 'to the prosperous homeland' (آباد an adjective 'prosperous/inhabited', بوم 'homeland').
segments.s453-g27: تو is the 2nd‑person address (poetic 'thou/you'). چندان here means 'to such an extent' = 'so much'. نوازش is an abstract noun 'kindness, favour'. بیابی is 2nd‑person singular present/subjunctive (used here as future-ish) 'you will obtain/receive/find'. ز شاه marks the source/agent 'from the king'.
segments.s453-g28: This segment is a simple listing of items introduced by repeated ز 'from/of' and linked by و 'and'. اسپ 'horse' (steed), مهر here means 'favour/gift' (king's favour), تیغ 'sword', and کلاه (here) 'helmet' — all objects of the king's bestowed نوازش. The repeated ز emphasizes each item as coming 'from' the king.
segments.s453-g29: همه شب 'all night' (همه = 'whole/entire' modifying شب). همی is the classical continuous/progressive particle ('kept on / continually'). لشکر is subject 'the army/host'. آراستند is past‑tense 3rd plural 'they adorned/arrayed/made ready', describing repeated/nightly activity.
segments.s453-g30: This clause puts the instrument/affected group first: سلیح (arms/weapons) and سواران (the riders/horsemen) with the verb بپیراستند (past 3rd plural). بپیراستند = they equipped/armed (the horsemen). The b‑ prefix is a classical verbal form; here the action is transitive 'they armed the riders'.
segments.s453-g31: چو = 'when'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. برزد سر is an archaic phrasing in which برزد (an older verbal form) + سر ('head') conveys 'raised/lifted its head' or 'appeared'. از introduces the source 'from' and تیره راغ is a poetic phrase 'dark gloom/black dusk' — راغ here is an archaic/poetic noun not directly represented in the supplied senses, so its listed senses don't match the usage.
segments.s453-g32: زمین 'the earth/ground' is the subject of شد 'became'. به کردار is a fixed classical phrase meaning 'in the likeness/manner of' (به + کردار = 'as/like'). روشن چراغ = 'a bright lamp' (روشن = bright, چراغ = lamp). The whole clause means 'the earth became like a bright lamp.'
segments.s453-g49: Literal word order places the numeral + title first, then the source phrase and the verb. چهل جاثلیق = 'forty Catholicoi' (a numeral + plural title). از دلیران is a genitival/partitive phrase 'from/out of the brave', and بکشت is an archaic past third‑person verb form ('he killed') with the subject omitted (common in epic narrative).
segments.s453-g50: بیامد is a simple past 'came'. صلیبی here names or describes a cruciform object/thing (used substantively/adjectivally). گرفته is a perfect/passive participle 'taken/seized' functioning adjectivally with صلیبی, and به مشت is a prepositional phrase ('into/in the fist' — مشت = 'fist'). The phrase describes someone arriving while clutching a crucifix.
segments.s453-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The sequence ز + و here represents the older contracted form ز او = 'from him/of him' (poetic contraction); رشنواد is a proper name (Roshnavad). آن شگفتی = 'that wonder/marvel', and بدید is the past 'saw'. The line thus sets a temporal scene 'When Roshnavad saw that marvel...'.
segments.s453-g52: ز شادی = 'from/because of joy' (a causal/source phrase). دل پهلوان = 'the hero's heart' (literal 'heart of the champion'), and بردمید is an older verb meaning 'was carried away'/'lifted up' (here passive/experiential: the heart is carried away by joy). The clause describes an emotional reaction.
segments.s453-g53: برو here is an exclamatory/imperative remnant related to 'go' but used in the narrative as part of the interjectional phrase that follows; آفرین is the interjection 'bravo' or 'well done', and کرد is the past 'did/said' (so: 'he said "Bravo!"'). و connects clauses, چندی is a duration 'for a while', and ستود is the past 'praised' — 'and he praised for a while.'
segments.s453-g54: بران = 'upon that' / 'thereupon' (linking back to the preceding acclamation). آفرین = 'the acclamation/praise', مهربانی = 'kindness/kind words', and فزود is a past form meaning 'increased' or 'added to'. Together: 'thereupon he increased (added) kind praise to the acclamation.'
segments.s453-g55: This is a sequence of short nominative/verb clauses: شب آمد = 'night came' (simple past). جهان قیرگون شد = 'the world became pitch‑black' (قیرگون = 'very dark/pitch‑black', شد = 'became'), and به رنگ ('in color') completes the descriptive phrase ('in hue'). The preposition به + رنگ expresses a change in appearance/colour.
segments.s453-g56: همی is a narrative/emphatic particle common in epic Persian (here marking the action that follows). بازگشتند is the past plural 'they returned'. یکسر can mean 'entirely' or 'at once' — here 'at once/all together' fits best. ز جنگ = 'from the battle'. The whole clause: 'they all returned at once from the battle.'
segments.s453-g89: Poetic clause: به قیصر is a dative phrase 'for/to the Caesar'; بر از کین functions adverbially 'on account of / because of hatred' (بر + از + noun); جهان (the grammatical subject) follows and تنگ شد is the verb phrase 'became narrow' (شد = past 'become'). Word order is topical/poetic: cause phrase precedes the subject and predicate.
segments.s453-g90: رخ نامدارانش is a noun phrase 'the faces/appearances of his famed ones' (نامدارانش = his renowned people); بی‌رنگ شد is a resultative: بی‌رنگ 'pale/faded' + شد 'became'. The verb is past 'became' and applies to the whole noun phrase.
segments.s453-g91: فرستاده آمد is a simple past clause 'a messenger came'; بر رشنواد uses بر as a directional/prepositional particle 'to/toward' with the proper name رشنواد ('to Reshnavad'). The phrase is a circumstantial/locative complement to آمد.
segments.s453-g92: که‌گر introduces an indirect conditional 'saying that if / that if ever'; دادگر is 'the just one' or 'just ruler'; سر نپیچد ز داد is idiomatic: سر نپیچد (نپَیچَد = negative present/subjunctive of پیچیدن) 'does not turn/avert his head' + ز داد 'from justice' = 'does not turn away from justice / does not abandon justice'. This is reported conditional reported by the messenger.
segments.s453-g93: شدند آنک is an impersonal/verb-initial narrative 'there became / it happened that' + آنک 'those who/that which'; جنگی بد modifies implied people or fighting—جنگی here = 'warlike/warrior' or 'military' and بد = 'bad/terrible'; از جنگ سیر is a prepositional phrase 'satiated/wearied from war' (سیر = 'sated/filled'). Together: 'there came to be those who, after a terrible war, were tired of fighting.'
segments.s453-g94: سر بخت روم is a genitive construction: سر 'head/top/chief' + بخت 'fortune/fate' + روم 'of Rome' = 'the chief fortune of Rome' or 'Rome's luck'; اندرآمد به زیر is a verbal phrase 'came under' or 'fell under' (اندرآمد = came in/into; به زیر = under), i.e. 'came under (their) control/sway.'
segments.s453-g95: که‌گر is conditional 'if'; باژ here = 'cry, signal, alarm' (a call); خواهید is 2nd person plural present/future 'you desire/will'; فرمان کنیم uses first-person-plural subjunctive/volitional 'we will order / we shall command' (کنیم expressing volition/intent). The clause offers to issue an order if they want a signal.
segments.s453-g96: بنوی is an imperative 'Write!' (addressing the addressee to put something down); یکی is the direct object 'one' (i.e. one message/letter); باز 'again' modifies the following verb; پیمان کنیم is first-person-plural subjunctive/volitional 'let us make / we will make a covenant'—together: 'Write one (message) and we will make a treaty again.'
segments.s453-g81: ببستند is a past, 3rd person plural verb (they closed/closed up). In classical/poetic Persian the verb often precedes its object, so گردان ایران میان is the noun phrase the verb applies to: گردان (troop/unit) modified by ایران (Iran) and followed by میان (’in the middle’). There is no ezâfe shown (typical in poetry) but the words form a single phrase 'the Iranian troop in the middle.'
segments.s453-g82: همی is a proclitic particle used in older/poetic Persian to mark continuing or iterative action (’kept on/was doing’). تاختند is past 3rd person plural (they charged/attacked). از پس is a prepositional phrase meaning 'after/behind' (in pursuit of), and رومیان is the object 'the Rûm/Romans.' Word order: particle + verb + prepositional phrase indicating whom they pursued.
segments.s453-g83: به here marks instrumentality ('with' / 'by'), so به شمشیر تیز means 'with a sharp sword' (شمشیر noun + تیز adjective; Persian places the adjective after the noun). آتش افروختند is a compound verbal idea: آتش (fire) + افروختند (past 3pl 'they kindled'), meaning 'they set fire (lit fires).' The past tense is perfective and the clause is an agentive action.
segments.s453-g84: همه شهرها را is a quantified, definite object: همه (all) + شهرها (cities/towns) with را marking the definite/direct object. همی again marks continuing action; سوختند is past 3rd person plural of سوزیدن/سوختن (they burned). Altogether: 'they burned all the towns.'
segments.s453-g85: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (often repeated for emphasis). روم و ز رومی repeats 'the land of Rum and the Rûm (its people),' stressing source and actors. برانگیخت is past tense 'arose/was stirred up' (3rd person singular here, an intransitive/impersonal usage), and گرد is an adverb meaning 'around/everywhere' (so 'a tumult rose around/from them'). Word order places the source phrase before the verb, then the adverbial گرد.
segments.s453-g86: کس here means 'no one' (negative sense). از introduces source/association ('from'); بوم و بر is a poetic pair meaning 'homeland and dwelling/place' (two words that together indicate 'home/land'); یاد is 'memory/thought' and دیگر 'other/else.' نکرد is past negative 3rd-person singular of کردن ('did not do') and here yields 'did not remember' or 'gave no thought to something else.' The whole clause says that nobody thought of anything else.
segments.s453-g87: خروشی is a nominal subject meaning 'a cry/clamour.' به زاری is an instrumental/qualifying phrase 'with lamentation' or 'a cry of lamentation' (به marking manner), and برآمد is past 3rd person singular 'arose/issued forth.' ز روم places the origin 'from Rum' after the verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s453-g88: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason ('for/because'). بگذاشتند is past 3rd person plural of گذاشتن with the prefix ب- here meaning 'they left/abandoned' (perfective completed action). آن is the demonstrative 'that/the,' دلارام is a proper name/epithet meaning 'Delaram' (the beloved/comfort), and بوم is 'homeland/native soil.' The clause therefore explains that the lament arose because they had abandoned that beloved of the homeland.
segments.s453-g9: همه (subject) + یک به دیگر (reciprocal phrase) + برآمیختند (past plural verb). The clause order is Subject — reciprocal adverbial — Verb: 'همه' (everyone) is the subject, 'یک به دیگر' literally 'one to another' marks mutual action, and 'برآمیختند' is the 3rd‑person plural past (archaic/poetic) meaning 'mingled/engaged each other'.
segments.s453-g10: چو introduces a simile ('like/as'); 'رود روان' is a noun + adjective ('flowing river/stream'); 'خون' is the direct object; 'همی' is an archaic progressive/continuous marker placed before the past verb; 'ریختند' is the past 3rd‑person plural of ریختن ('poured/spilled'). Altogether: 'like a flowing river they were pouring blood.'
segments.s453-g11: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; 'داراب' is the proper name (subject of the implied clause); 'دید' is the past tense verb 'saw/beheld'; 'آن سپاه نبرد' is the object phrase 'that battle‑host' (سپاه 'army/host' modified by نبرد 'battle', i.e. 'army engaged in battle').
segments.s453-g12: 'به پیش اندر آمد' is a set phrase meaning 'came forward/in front' (به پیش = to the front; اندر = into; آمد = came). The following short phrase 'به کردار گرد' states purpose/manner: به کردار = 'in/for the doing of deeds' (کردار = deed, action), and گرد here functions as an adverb 'about/around' or 'to go about' — together implying he entered the fray to go about performing deeds (poetic, concise word order).
segments.s453-g13: ازان = 'of/from that' introducing the group affected; 'لشکر روم' = 'the Roman army/host'; 'چندان' = 'to such an extent/so many'; 'بکشت' is an archaic past form meaning 'he killed' (ب‑ prefix with past stem) — overall: 'he killed so many of that Roman host.'
segments.s453-g14: که introduces the clause of result/exclamation ('that...'); گفتی here is an idiomatic/poetic use meaning 'one would say' or 'as they say' (not a literal noun), introducing the proverbial image; 'فلک تیغ دارد به مشت' is literal: 'the sky/heaven (فلک) has (دارد) a sword (تیغ) in its fist (به مشت)' — a hyperbolic expression implying fate or heaven wields a sword. دارد is present indicative 3rd singular.
segments.s453-g15: همی رفت is a progressive past 'he kept going/advancing' (همی as continuous marker + رفت past of رفتن). زان گونه = 'in that manner/that way' (زان = from that; گونه = manner), بر سان شیر = 'like a lion' (بر/برِ + سان = 'in the likeness of/like', شیر = 'lion'). The clause is a simile describing his manner of attack.
segments.s453-g16: This is a second simile built as a noun with successive positionals: 'نهنگی' (whale/whale‑like being) followed by 'به چنگ اژدهایی' ('in the claw/grip of a dragon' — به چنگ marks being in the grasp, and اژدهایی is 'of a dragon') and 'به زیر' ('below/beneath'). Grammatically these prepositional elements modify نهنگی, giving the image 'like a whale beneath the claw of a dragon.'
segments.s453-g1: بگفت is the literary past-tense verb 'he said' (verb before object is common in Ferdowsi). این و زان is a paired object/topic phrase 'this and that' (این = 'this', زان = 'from that/that'), so the whole chunk reads 'he said this and that' with برگرفت beginning the next coordinated action.
segments.s453-g2: This clause gives the range and the action: ازان مرز تا روم is a prepositional/range phrase 'from that border as far as Rome' (تا = 'as far as'). لشکر is the thing mustered ('an army') and گرفت is the simple past 'took/assembled' — subject is omitted (he). Word order puts the range phrase up front, then the nominal لشکر, finishing with the verb گرفت.
segments.s453-g3: سپهبد is the subject 'spahbed/commander'. طلایه is the direct object 'the vanguard' and به داراب ('to Darab') marks the recipient; داد is the past tense 'gave'. The simple S–O–(to X)–V order is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s453-g4: Here طلایه is fronted as a topic (repeating 'the vanguard'). سنان را is the definite direct object marked by را ('Sanan' receives something). به زهر آب names what was given — literally 'with/poison water' = 'a draught of poisoned water' — and داد is again the past 'gave.' So the clause means '(as for) the vanguard, (he) gave Sanan poisoned water.'
segments.s453-g5: هم‌انگه is an adverbial 'at that moment/then'. طلایه is the subject 'the vanguard' and بیامد is the past 'came'; ز is the poetic short form of از meaning 'from', so ز روم = 'from Rome.' Word order is subject + verb with the source introduced by the preposition z.
segments.s453-g6: This is a nominal apposition describing that arriving vanguard: وزین سو is an adjectival + 'side/part' phrase (here 'weighty/dignified' + 'side' → 'a dignified presence/figure'), نگهدار is a noun 'guardian', and این مرز و بوم is the object of guardianship 'this border and homeland.' No finite verb here; the phrase apposes the previous clause.
segments.s453-g7: زناگه here functions idiomatically as 'thereupon/at that time' (a fusion of ز آن گاه in classical style). دو لشکر بهم is 'two armies at/against each other' (بهم = 'to one another'), and بازخورد is the past action meaning they struck/turned on one another — 'clashed' or 'fell upon each other.' Note: the provided lemma senses list for زناگه is not the usage here, so sense mapping below marks that.
segments.s453-g8: برآمد is the past 'arose/was raised.' هم‌آنگاه again is 'at that moment/straightaway.' گرد نبرد is a nominal phrase 'the dust/circle of battle' (گرد here means the dust or swirling circle associated with conflict, modified by نبرد 'battle'). The clause says that immediately the battle's dust rose.
segments.s453-g57: سپهبد is the subject (a military title ‘spahbed’). به + لشکرگه رومیان is a locative/goal phrase: به here means 'to/at' and لشکرگه (army-camp) takes the genitive رومیان (‘of the Romans’) after it, so the phrase means 'at the Roman camp'. Word order is subject + locative phrase.
segments.s453-g58: برآسود is a past/inchoative verb meaning 'rested/was at rest' (verb precedes the coordination). و simply links the two actions. بگشاد is an archaic past form meaning 'opened/loosened' (the b‑ prefix is the classical past marker here). بند میان is the direct object: بند 'fetter/bond/barrier' and میان 'between' (postposed), together 'the barrier/tie between (them)'.
segments.s453-g59: ببخشید here is a past-tense verb meaning 'granted/bestowed' (classical b‑ form of بخشیدن used transitively). در شب is a temporal phrase 'during the night'. بسی خواسته is a noun phrase where بسی (classical intensifier 'much/very/many') modifies خواسته 'requests/wishes', so 'many requests'. The order is verb + temporal phrase + object.
segments.s453-g60: شد is the past of شدن used here as 'became/was made' (resultative/passive sense). از is the preposition 'from/out of'. خواسته refers to 'what had been asked for' (the requested supplies), and لشکر آراسته is a nominative/subject phrase 'the army, made ready' with آراسته an adjective/participle meaning 'equipped/arranged'. The whole clause means 'and from the requested things the army was equipped.'
segments.s453-g61: فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent'. نزدیک داراب is a locative/adverbial phrase 'near Darab' (نزدیک used with a place name). کس is an indefinite object 'someone' ('he sent someone to/near Darab'). The verb-first word order is typical in this epic diction.
segments.s453-g62: که‌ای functions as a relative/introduction to an appositive description ('who is/— namely'). It introduces the descriptive phrase that follows. شیردل مرد فریادرس is a stacked epithet: شیردل 'lion‑hearted' (adjective), مرد 'man' (noun, here 'hero/valiant man'), and فریادرس 'one who brings succour/rescuer' — together describing the person sent.
segments.s453-g63: نگه کن is an imperative construction: نگه (a noun 'look/watch') + کن (imperative 'do') functioning as 'look!' or 'wait!'. کنون is the adverb 'now'. تا introduces a subordinate clause 'so that / in order that', here 'to see what your choice is'. پسند تو چیست is a copular question 'what is your liking/choice?': پسند 'preference', تو 'you', چیست 'is what'.
segments.s453-g64: وزی is an older/classical fused conjunctional phrase (و + زی) meaning roughly 'and concerning this / and from that' (classical usage introducing an additional question about the matter); it functions to connect and introduce the next question. خواسته سودمند تو چیست is another copular question: خواسته 'wish/request', سودمند 'useful/profitable' (adjective postposed), تو 'your', چیست 'is what' — 'what useful request of yours is it?'.

sentences.s453-s49: Caesar sent things of every kind, and also many beautiful slave‑girls.
sentences.s453-s50: The spahbed accepted from him whatever there was, and he was not displeased with the dinars and jewels.
sentences.s453-s21: From that spot he turned to the right and brought some weapons and small bundles.
sentences.s453-s22: The whole Roman host was put to disorder; no one among the heroes saw his own self.
sentences.s453-s23: The brave men of Iran, behaving like lions, still charged, pursuing into the midst of the brave.
sentences.s453-s24: They killed so many of the Roman host that the battlefield's soil became a flower from blood.
sentences.s453-s9: Thus he raced up to the Romans' camp, charging like a living lion.
sentences.s453-s10: The ground, because of the Romans, became like a sea of blood; for the ambitious man the sword became his guide.
sentences.s453-s11: He returned from the Romans with victory and came near the commander Gerdnefaraz.
sentences.s453-s12: He received much praise from Reshnvad, saying, 'May this royal army never be without you.'
sentences.s453-s17: Both hosts struck at one another; a darkness gathered around the radiant sun.
sentences.s453-s18: When Darab came forward and launched an attack, he entrusted the reins to his bold steed.
sentences.s453-s19: There was no one left before the Roman ranks; from the squadrons of swordsmen hardly any remained.
sentences.s453-s20: He entered into the very heart of the host like a wolf and scattered that great army.
sentences.s453-s37: When the face of the sun darkened in the west, the sky put on a dark brocade.
sentences.s453-s38: That same guard passed out of the dark night; the vanguard, scattered, was around the plain.
sentences.s453-s39: The sentries' uproar arose like an earthquake; indeed it became like the cry of a wild lion.
sentences.s453-s40: When the sun lifted its golden shield, the warriors raised their heads from sleep.
sentences.s453-s33: Keep/hold whatever pleases your counsel; give (away) that which your heart's guide brings to you.
sentences.s453-s34: Whatever of that does not please you, give it up; you are more famed than the master of Rakhsh.
sentences.s453-s35: When he saw that, Darab became joyful; someone took up a spear for the sake of fame.
sentences.s453-s36: He sent another toward Reshnawad; he said to him, 'May you be victorious and joyful.'
sentences.s453-s13: When we return from this campaign against Rome, the army will come into the prosperous homeland.
sentences.s453-s14: You will receive so great a favour from the king — horses, favours/gifts, and swords and helmets.
sentences.s453-s15: All night long the host made ready; they arrayed the horsemen with arms.
sentences.s453-s16: When the sun lifted its head from the dark gloom, the earth became like a bright lamp.
sentences.s453-s25: He killed forty Catholicoi from among the brave; then (someone) came, a cruciform object clenched in his fist.
sentences.s453-s26: When Roshnavad saw that wonder, the champion's heart was carried away with joy.
sentences.s453-s27: He cried 'Bravo!' and praised (him) for a while; upon that acclamation he increased his kind praise.
sentences.s453-s28: Night came; the world became pitch-black in hue, and they all returned from the battle at once.
sentences.s453-s45: Because of that grudge the world grew narrow for the Caesar; the faces of his renowned men grew pale.
sentences.s453-s46: A messenger came to Reshnavad, saying that if the just one does not turn his head away from justice.
sentences.s453-s47: There arose those who, after a bitter war, were wearied of fighting; Rome's fortune fell under (their) sway.
sentences.s453-s48: If you wish for a signal/cry, we will give the order; write one (message) and we will renew the covenant.
sentences.s453-s41: They closed up the Iranian ranks in the center and kept charging after the Rûm (the Romans/Byzantines).
sentences.s453-s42: With sharp swords they kindled fire; they burned all the towns.
sentences.s453-s43: From Rum and from the Rûm a tumult rose all around; no one from home or homeland thought of anything else.
sentences.s453-s44: A cry — a lamentation — arose from Rum, for they had abandoned that Delaram of the homeland.
sentences.s453-s5: All fell upon one another; like a flowing river they poured out blood.
sentences.s453-s6: When Darab saw that the host was in battle, he came forward into the fray to go about performing deeds.
sentences.s453-s7: He killed so many of that Roman host that, as one might say, the heavens hold a sword in their fist.
sentences.s453-s8: He continued in that manner like a lion; like a whale beneath the claw of a dragon.
sentences.s453-s1: He said this and that, and from that frontier as far as Rome he mustered an army.
sentences.s453-s2: The spahbed gave the vanguard to Darab; (and) he gave Sanan a draught of poisoned water.
sentences.s453-s3: At that moment the vanguard came from Rome — a dignified guardian of this frontier and homeland.
sentences.s453-s4: Thereupon the two armies fell upon one another; at once the dust (circle) of battle rose.
sentences.s453-s29: The spahbed rested at the Roman camp and loosed/opened the barrier between them.
sentences.s453-s30: That night many requests were granted, and from what was requested the army was equipped.
sentences.s453-s31: He sent someone near Darab, — a lion‑hearted man, a succorer.
sentences.s453-s32: Look now, to see what your preference is, and what useful request you have.
